## New Patriot Air and Missile Defense System Ready by 2028

**Date:** Thu, 10 Apr 2025 20:04:53 +0000  
**Author:** norskluftvern  
**Type:** Post  
**Link:** [http://norskluftvern.com/2025/04/10/new-patriot-air-and-missile-defense-system-ready-by-2028/](http://norskluftvern.com/2025/04/10/new-patriot-air-and-missile-defense-system-ready-by-2028/)

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The Patriot system has long served as a cornerstone of U.S. air defense, intercepting aircraft, cruise missiles, and ballistic missiles. However, with adversaries developing faster and more sophisticated threats, the system is being overhauled.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The next-generation Patriot Air and Missile Defense System, which is on track to be operational by 2028, embodies the fusion of advanced radars, improved interceptor missiles, and an integrated command-and-control network that can seamlessly coordinate sensor data and engagement responses across multiple domains.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>This article provides a comprehensive view of the modernization process, technical advancements, and strategic export prospects of the new Patriot system and its key integrated components.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Advanced Sensors: LTAMDS GhostEye Radar</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>A crucial element of Patriot’s modernization is the integration of the LTAMDS GhostEye radar. Unlike older radar systems such as the AN/MPQ-65/MPQ-53, GhostEye is an active electronically scanned array (AESA) radar featuring three overlapping 120° fixed arrays. This configuration provides seamless 360° coverage, eliminating blind spots and allowing the radar to track small, fast-moving targets with extraordinary accuracy. Its gallium nitride (GaN) power amplifiers not only increase sensitivity and resolution but also improve power efficiency. The GhostEye radar feeds real-time, high-fidelity sensor data into the integrated network, thereby boosting the overall performance of the missile defense system. </p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The goal is to integrate four GhostEye radars per battalion, bolstering missile defense capabilities for both domestic and forward-deployed forces.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph {"className":""} -->
<p><strong>Key Features</strong>:</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:list -->
<ul class="wp-block-list"><!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>360° Coverage</strong>: Utilizes three fixed 120° arrays that overlap to eliminate blind spots and maintain continuous tracking of aerial threats.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>High-Fidelity Detection</strong>: Employs state-of-the-art gallium nitride (GaN) power amplifiers for superior resolution, improved sensitivity, and enhanced target discrimination—even for small, fast-moving objects.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Rapid Tracking &amp; Data Accuracy</strong>: Capable of delivering precise measurements of target shape, size, distance, and speed, which is critical for effective missile intercepts.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item --></ul>
<!-- /wp:list -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Networked Command with the Integrated Battle Command System (IBCS)</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Complementing advanced sensors and interceptors is the U.S. Army’s Integrated Battle Command System (IBCS). IBCS is designed on a “connect any sensor to any shooter” principle, which means that data from various platforms are fused into one actionable, real-time operational picture. By facilitating interoperability between legacy and next-generation systems, IBCS dramatically improves decision-making speeds and coordinated engagement, which is critical for countering high-speed, multi-domain threats. </p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The system has completed rigorous initial operational test and evaluation (IOT&amp;E) exercises and has been approved for full-rate production as of 2023.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph {"className":""} -->
<p><strong>Key Features</strong>:</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:list -->
<ul class="wp-block-list"><!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Data Fusion &amp; Integration</strong>: IBCS collects and fuses data from diverse sensor sources—including Patriot radars (AN/MPQ-65/MPQ-53/GhostEye), THAAD’s AN/TPY-2, and naval radars (AN/SPY-1/AN/SPY-6)—into a single, actionable operational picture.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Interoperability</strong>: It allows disparate defense systems to share real-time tracking data and engage targets cooperatively, enhancing the effectiveness of kinetic intercept solutions.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Real-Time Engagement</strong>: With rapid target identification and prioritization, IBCS supports swift decision-making in high-threat environments, ensuring effective countermeasures against ballistic missiles, cruise missiles, and UAVs.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item --></ul>
<!-- /wp:list -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">PAC-3: The Interceptor at the Forefront</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The modernized Patriot incorporates advanced missiles—most notably the PAC-3 interceptor in its MSE (Missile Segment Enhancement) variant—which boasts extended range, superior agility, and improved guidance systems. This upgrade enhances the system’s hit-to-kill capability, ensuring that targets are neutralized quickly and effectively during their terminal flight phase.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The Patriot PAC-3 missile, especially in its enhanced MSE variant, forms the kinetic heart of the modernized air defense system. Utilizing a dual-pulse solid rocket motor, PAC-3 MSE is capable of decelerating and then reaccelerating during the terminal phase, thereby increasing its maneuverability and hit probability. With advanced inertial navigation combined with a high-precision data link, the PAC-3 interceptors adjust their flight path in real time based on incoming sensor updates. These improvements not only bolster the defensive shield for assets but also enable integration with broader missile defense networks, including those operating under IBCS.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph {"className":""} -->
<p><strong>Key Features</strong>:</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:list -->
<ul class="wp-block-list"><!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Hit-to-Kill Technology</strong>: PAC-3 uses kinetic energy to destroy its target by direct impact rather than relying on explosive warheads.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Enhanced Guidance and Control</strong>: The missile incorporates an advanced inertial navigation system combined with a high-precision data link, which provides real-time updates during flight for improved target tracking and engagement.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Dual-Pulse Rocket Motor</strong>: The propulsion system is optimized to allow the missile to slow down and then reaccelerate during the terminal phase, increasing its maneuverability and kill probability.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Upgraded Variants (PAC-3 MSE)</strong>: The Missile Segment Enhancement (MSE) upgrade improves range, maneuverability, and kill capacity. This version includes enhanced electronics and propulsion features to counter emerging missile threats more effectively.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item --></ul>
<!-- /wp:list -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Prospective Exports and Global Impact</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Beyond its domestic deployment, the new Patriot system and its modernized components are attracting substantial international interest. Several prospective exports underline the system’s global strategic importance:</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:list -->
<ul class="wp-block-list"><!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Germany</strong> is poised to secure a deal for PAC-3 MSE missiles, with an estimated sale of $5 billion approved by U.S. authorities.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Spain</strong> and the <strong>Netherlands</strong> have entered contracts for modernized Patriot fire units and associated equipment, reinforcing their integrated air defense.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Poland</strong> received in September 2023 approval for a potential $4 billion purchase of Patriot Configuration-3+ systems equipped with the Integrated Air and Missile Defense Battle Command System (IBCS).</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Bahrain</strong>:&nbsp;On March 5, 2024, Bahrain received its first shipment of Patriot PAC-3 MSE interceptors, enhancing its air and missile defense capabilities.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Kuwait</strong> recently obtained U.S. State Department approval for a $400 million upgrade and recertification package for its Patriot missiles.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>United Arab Emirates (UAE)</strong>:&nbsp;The UAE has been authorized to procure 452 PAC-3 MSE missiles and related equipment, with the deal estimated at $2.728 billion.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>European Consortium</strong>:&nbsp;In January 2024, Germany, the Netherlands, Spain, and Romania collectively ordered up to 1,000 Patriot Guidance Enhanced Missiles (GEM-T) through a $5.5 billion contract facilitated by NATO's Support and Procurement Agency.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item --></ul>
<!-- /wp:list -->

<!-- wp:separator -->
<hr class="wp-block-separator has-alpha-channel-opacity" />
<!-- /wp:separator -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Sources</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:list {"ordered":true} -->
<ol class="wp-block-list"><!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><a href="https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integrated_Air_and_Missile_Defense" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Information on LTAMDS GhostEye radar and integrated air and missile defense.</a> </li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integrated_Air_and_Missile_Defense_Battle_Command_System" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Details on the Integrated Battle Command System (IBCS) and its role in missile defense.</a></li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><a href="https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/MIM-104_Patriot" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Overview of the Patriot PAC-3 system and its advanced interceptor capabilities.</a></li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><a href="https://www.reuters.com/world/can-us-missile-defense-system-shield-guam-chinese-threat-2024-12-11/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Reports on U.S. missile defense deployments and prospective export deals.</a></li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li>Additional articles from Reuters, Defense News, and official U.S. Army/Missile Defense Agency publications for insights on contracts, testing, and export negotiations.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item --></ol>
<!-- /wp:list -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->

<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

## Iron Beam: Laser Innovation Redefining Short-Range Air Defenses

**Date:** Fri, 11 Apr 2025 20:11:16 +0000  
**Author:** norskluftvern  
**Type:** Post  
**Link:** [http://norskluftvern.com/2025/04/11/iron-beam-laser-innovation-redefining-short-range-air-defenses-and-global-markets/](http://norskluftvern.com/2025/04/11/iron-beam-laser-innovation-redefining-short-range-air-defenses-and-global-markets/)

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The modern battlefield is witnessing a rapid evolution in threats—from inexpensive rockets and mortars to agile unmanned aerial systems (UAVs) and loitering munitions. In response, Israel’s defense industry has pioneered directed energy solutions, among which the Iron Beam system stands out as a revolutionary laser-based air defense tool. Developed by Rafael Advanced Defense Systems, Iron Beam promises an efficient, low-cost method of neutralizing short-range airborne threats almost instantaneously.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>This article provides a comprehensive review of the Iron Beam system—its development, deployment strategies, export opportunities, and the criticisms that add nuance to its role in modern air defense.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Development: A Laser Solution to Short-Range Threats</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Iron Beam represents a paradigm shift in air defense technology. Unlike traditional kinetic missile interceptors that incur significant cost per engagement, Iron Beam employs a high-energy laser to destroy incoming threats with near-zero cost per shot. </p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"level":3} -->
<h3 class="wp-block-heading">Key developmental milestones</h3>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Concept and Innovation</strong>:<br />Conceived in the early 2010s, Iron Beam was designed as a complement to Israel’s layered defense systems such as Iron Dome. Its primary mission is to intercept short-range projectiles—including rockets, artillery shells, mortars, and UAVs—in their final trajectory.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Technical Specifications</strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Laser Power</strong>: The system is engineered with a laser capable of delivering hundreds of kilowatts of power within a controlled energy pulse.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Effective Range and Reaction Time</strong>: With an interception range of a few kilometers (typically up to 5 km in ideal conditions), Iron Beam offers a near-instantaneous response time measured in seconds.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Precision and Cost-effectiveness</strong>: By precisely depositing energy onto a target to either disrupt its guidance or cause structural failure, the system provides a cost-effective alternative to interceptor missiles.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Testing and Validation</strong>:<br />Extensive field tests have demonstrated the laser’s ability to neutralize small rockets and drones. Independent evaluations suggest that, under optimal weather conditions, Iron Beam can engage multiple targets sequentially with repeatable performance.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Deployment: Integrating Laser Defense into the Shield</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>While Iron Beam remains in the advanced testing and pre-deployment phases, plans for its integration are already underway.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Operational Integration with Existing Systems</strong>:<br />The Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) are evaluating Iron Beam to complement the Iron Dome system. While Iron Dome is highly effective against larger rockets and missiles, Iron Beam’s rapid response capability and minimal per-engagement cost make it ideal for countering low-cost, high-volume threats.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Mobility and Deployment Platforms</strong>:<br />The system is designed to be mounted on mobile platforms—truck-based or fixed installations—allowing rapid redeployment based on threat dynamics. Its compact design and modular nature mean that multiple units can be fielded to create overlapping defensive sectors.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Environmental and Operational Considerations</strong>:<br />Although the laser system operates optimally in clear atmospheric conditions, deployment strategies are accounting for potential limitations due to adverse weather (e.g., heavy fog or rain). Engineers continue refining power management and cooling systems to mitigate such challenges.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Export Prospects: A Global Interest in Directed Energy Defense</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Iron Beam’s innovative approach has captured the attention of international defense communities, with several countries showing interest in acquiring or co-developing similar systems. Prospective export considerations:</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Market Appeal</strong>:<br />The promise of extremely low per-engagement costs and rapid target neutralization makes Iron Beam an attractive proposition for nations facing asymmetric threats. Its potential to complement existing multi-layer air defense systems is particularly appealing.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Potential Export Customers</strong>:<br />Countries in the Middle East, Southeast Asia, and even parts of Europe have expressed interest in directed energy systems. Some regional allies have already held discussions with Israeli officials regarding technology transfers, joint development initiatives, or direct procurement.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Challenges for Export</strong>:<br />Despite strong interest, potential buyers are cautious about issues such as system efficacy under varied environmental conditions and the necessary infrastructure investments for laser systems. Moreover, export success depends on overcoming technological integration challenges and meeting specific national defense standards.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Criticism and Future Outlook</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>While Iron Beam is hailed as a game changer for short-range air defense, it is not without its critics.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Weather Limitations</strong>:<br />Laser-based systems can be less effective in adverse atmospheric conditions. Critics argue that cloud cover, fog, or precipitation might significantly reduce laser beam performance.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Saturation and Redundancy Concerns</strong>:<br />Although Iron Beam offers rapid engagement, questions remain about its performance under saturation attacks involving swarms of low-cost UAVs or rockets. Some defense analysts caution that laser systems might need to be coupled with traditional interceptors to cover all threat envelopes.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Integration and Cost Considerations</strong>:<br />While the per-shot cost is minimal, overall system acquisition, maintenance, and integration with existing defense networks represent substantial investments. There are also concerns that the complexity of synchronizing laser defense with multi-layer systems may lead to logistical or interoperability challenges.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Despite these criticisms, the potential benefits—especially in terms of cost-efficiency and rapid response—position Iron Beam as a valuable component of a diversified, layered air defense strategy. Future refinements and field applications will determine its role within integrated defense systems globally.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Conclusion</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Iron Beam is at the forefront of directed energy defense innovation, offering a revolutionary solution to the growing threat of short-range projectile attacks. As Israel advances its testing and moves toward operational deployment, international interest in exporting similar capabilities continues to grow. While challenges remain regarding environmental limitations and integration complexity, ongoing developments promise to refine and overcome these obstacles, paving the way for Iron Beam to become a vital asset in modern air and missile defense strategies worldwide.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:separator -->
<hr class="wp-block-separator has-alpha-channel-opacity" />
<!-- /wp:separator -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Sources</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:list {"ordered":true} -->
<ol class="wp-block-list"><!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Rafael Advanced Defense Systems Official Website</strong> – Detailed technical information and developmental history of the Iron Beam system.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Reuters Articles</strong> – Coverage on Israeli defense modernization efforts and export interest in advanced defense technologies.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Defense News and GlobalSecurity.org</strong> – Reporting on field test results, system performance metrics, and critiques of directed energy systems.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Jane’s Information Group</strong> – Analysis of cost implications, integration challenges, and comparative assessments of laser versus kinetic interceptor technologies.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>NATO and European Defense Publications</strong> – Studies on the integration of new defense technologies with existing multilayer air defense networks.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><strong>Industry Expert Analyses</strong> – Commentary and assessments from defense analysts on the operational and export prospects for directed energy systems like Iron Beam.</li>
<!-- /wp:list-item --></ol>
<!-- /wp:list -->

## Redefining the Battlefield: How Wide Proliferation of High Powered Laser Air Defenses can Transform Operations

**Date:** Sat, 12 Apr 2025 12:08:40 +0000  
**Author:** norskluftvern  
**Type:** Post  
**Link:** [http://norskluftvern.com/2025/04/12/redefining-the-battlefield-how-wide-proliferation-of-high-powered-laser-air-defenses-can-transform-operations/](http://norskluftvern.com/2025/04/12/redefining-the-battlefield-how-wide-proliferation-of-high-powered-laser-air-defenses-can-transform-operations/)

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>As high powered laser weapon systems mature and become widely deployed, the very nature of the battlefield is set to change dramatically. The adoption of directed-energy systems such as the U.S. Army’s DE M-SHORAD, Israel’s Iron Beam, and the U.K.’s DragonFire offers not only a revolutionary method for countering short-range air threats but also paves the way for systemic changes across doctrine, logistics, and tactical operations. In a landscape where Ukraine’s recent success in shooting down thousands of hostile aerial vehicles highlights the importance of advanced air defense, laser air defenses represents a potential game changer for both national defense and global exports. Laser air defenses when deployed in large numbers, have the potential to fundamentally reshape both defensive and offensive operations in the battlefields of the not so distant future.&nbsp; </p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Estimated basic characteristics</strong> of common laser air defense weapons in a not to distant future battlefield:  </p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Laser Power</strong>: Capable of delivering around hundred kilowatts of energy in short pulses, sufficient to destroy small incoming projectiles.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Effective Range</strong>: Typically effective up to approximately 5 kilometers under optimal conditions.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Reaction Time</strong>: Capable of engaging targets within 2–5 seconds of detection.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Cost Efficiency</strong>: With a cost per engagement potentially as low as a few cents versus millions of dollars per missile intercept, laser weapons offer a transformative economic advantage.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Field tests have confirmed the potential, demonstrating successful interception of various targets in controlled environments. Yet, despite the promise, this class of weapons are still being refined to mitigate environmental limitations, such as performance degradation in fog, rain, or dust conditions.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>The following analysis</strong> outlines the likely impacts on both defensive and offensive operations in each side of a conflict.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"className":""} -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Overall Battlefield Redefinition</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph {"className":""} -->
<p>In a future where such laser air defenses are prolific, the battlefield would be characterized by:</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>High-Speed Engagement Networks</strong>: Both sides would operate integrated, sensor-driven networks with near-instantaneous response times, reducing the time available for adversary decision-making.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Economic Rebalancing</strong>: The dramatic cost differential between low-cost laser engagements and expensive missile interceptors would influence budget priorities, with defensive forces potentially reallocating resources toward sustaining and upgrading directed-energy capabilities.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Inter-Domain Shifts</strong>: Offensive operations may increasingly turn to unconventional domains (cyber, electronic warfare, space) in an effort to bypass or overwhelm state-of-the-art laser defenses, leading to a more multidimensional approach to warfare.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Doctrine and Training Transformation</strong>: Both defenders and attackers would have to update their tactics and training regimens to incorporate the advantages and limitations of directed-energy systems. Rapid, adaptive operations and resilient, redundant command-and-control networks would become central to military planning.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"className":""} -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Impact on Defensive Operations</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:heading {"level":3,"className":""} -->
<h3 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>Rapid and Continuous Engagement</strong></h3>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Faster Reaction Times</strong>: Laser systems can neutralize threats in a matter of seconds. This rapid engagement minimizes the window of vulnerability and allows defenders to quickly respond to a barrage of incoming projectiles, whether they are short-range rockets, artillery shells, or drones.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Cost Efficiency and Sustainability</strong>: With each laser shot costing a fraction of the expense of a kinetic interceptor, forces employing prolific laser weapons can afford to engage numerous low-cost threats repeatedly, reducing the strain on their interceptor inventories during prolonged or saturation attacks.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"level":3,"className":""} -->
<h3 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>Integration into Multi-Layer Defense Architectures</strong></h3>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Sensor Network Fusion</strong>: Laser systems are typically integrated with advanced sensor networks and real-time command and control systems. This integration enhances situational awareness by providing a unified operational picture. Consequently, defensive forces can coordinate their responses, assigning laser engagements for low-cost or high-volume targets while reserving traditional interceptors for strategic, high-value enemy munitions.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Mobility and Flexibility</strong>: Many modern laser systems are designed to be mounted on mobile platforms. This mobility allows defenders to reposition quickly based on evolving threat corridors or to cover gaps in fixed defenses, thereby increasing overall battlefield resilience.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"level":3,"className":""} -->
<h3 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>Operational Adaptability</strong></h3>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Adaptive Defense Posture</strong>: The capability to rapidly engage multiple threats encourages a flexible defensive posture. Commanders may shift away from static, area-denial strategies to more dynamic, responsive methods of protecting critical assets. Integrated laser systems might be used in combination with kinetic interceptors, establishing a multi-layered shield that adjusts to different threat types and densities.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Reduced Attrition of Expensive Missiles</strong>: With lasers handling a significant share of incoming low-cost targets, the overall consumption of expensive missile interceptors could be reduced. This resource preservation may enhance the sustainability of defensive operations over extended conflicts.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"className":""} -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Impact on Offensive Operations</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:heading {"level":3,"className":""} -->
<h3 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>Adversary Countermeasures and Increased Complexity</strong></h3>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Adapting to High-Volume Defenses</strong>: Knowing that lasers provide near-instantaneous, continuous air defense, offensive forces may revise their tactics. Traditional massed missile or drone attacks might become less effective, forcing attackers to either increase the volume of ordnance or develop sophisticated countermeasures to overcome the rapid engagement capabilities.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Diversification of Attack Profiles</strong>: Offensively, adversaries could shift toward more stealthy or low-observable methods to avoid detection by advanced sensor networks or may employ decoys and electronic warfare techniques to confuse laser systems. This evolution would require attackers to invest in advanced countermeasures and to reallocate resources to overcome the high-speed, layered defenses.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"level":3,"className":""} -->
<h3 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>Extended Operational Planning and Resource Allocation</strong></h3>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Balancing Force Structure</strong>: As defenders rely more on lasers, offensive planners may need to reconsider their own force structure to maintain an effective strike capability. This could include developing alternative delivery platforms that exploit other domains (such as cyber or space) to bypass robust laser defenses.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Cost Considerations on the Attack Side</strong>: The low cost and high repetition rate of laser defenses might compel offensive forces to either invest in a significantly larger arsenal of cheap, disposable munitions (to overwhelm the defensive system) or redirect funds toward sophisticated stealth and electronic warfare technologies.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"level":3,"className":""} -->
<h3 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>Impact on Strategic Deterrence</strong></h3>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Increased Deterrence Effect</strong>: A highly effective, continuous air defense shield based on lasers could act as a strong deterrent. Offensively, potential adversaries may be discouraged from launching large-scale aerial attacks if they are confident that even low-cost, high-volume threats will be neutralized rapidly.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Shifting Offensive Priorities</strong>: Should laser defenses prove robust, offensive strategies might pivot toward alternative domains. For example, attackers might increase reliance on cyber operations to disrupt the integrated sensor and command networks, or adopt ground-based and maritime strategies that target the logistical infrastructure supporting these defenses.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Image: Northrop Grumman</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>AI-assisted article.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

---

## IRIS-T SL vs. NASAMS: Air Defense Systems Comparison

**Date:** Tue, 15 Apr 2025 17:22:33 +0000  
**Author:** norskluftvern  
**Type:** Post  
**Link:** [http://norskluftvern.com/2025/04/15/iris-t-sl-vs-nasams-air-defense-systems-comparison/](http://norskluftvern.com/2025/04/15/iris-t-sl-vs-nasams-air-defense-systems-comparison/)

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The <a href="https://norskluftvern.com/2025/02/08/nasams-norwegian-advanced-surface-to-air-missile-system-mobilt-og-modulaert-middelsrekkende-luftvernsystem/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">NASAMS</a> and the <a href="https://norskluftvern.com/2025/02/09/iris-t-sl/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">IRIS-T SL</a> are both modern ground‐based air defence solutions used by NATO and partner nations, but differ in terms of architecture, primary interceptor missiles and sensors and operational concepts.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>IRIS-T SL</strong>: Infra Red Imaging System Tail/Thrust Vector Controlled – Surface Launched.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Developed by the German company Diehl Defence, evolved from the IRIS-T air-to-air missile—a highly agile, short-range interceptor originally developed to replace older AIM-9 Sidewinders. Its ground-based variants are based on the same missile technology, and commonly designated as SLS for the short-range configuration and SLM for the medium-range version.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:image {"id":3042,"sizeSlug":"large"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img src="https://norskluftvern.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/img_7356-1.jpg?w=768" alt="" class="wp-image-3042" /><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Graphic: Diehl Defence</figcaption></figure>
<!-- /wp:image -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>IRIS-T SL system is designed for integration with various radar systems and command and control centers, allowing for flexibility based on national requirements and existing defense infrastructure.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>However, IRIS-T SL batteries can be integrated with different radar systems such as HENSOLDT TRML-4D, Saab Giraffe series and the CEA CEAFAR. Moreover the batteries are coupled with modern battle management systems, where one alternative is the Fortion IBMS (Integrated Battle Management Software) from Airbus. Another marketed solution is the Lockheed Martin system named SkyKeeper.&nbsp;</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:image {"id":3044,"sizeSlug":"large"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img src="https://norskluftvern.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/img_7357-1.jpg?w=1024" alt="" class="wp-image-3044" /><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">IRIS-T SLM unit with tactical operations centre and 3D radar. Image: Diehl Defence</figcaption></figure>
<!-- /wp:image -->

<!-- wp:image {"id":3052,"sizeSlug":"large"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img src="https://norskluftvern.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/img_7358-1.jpg?w=1024" alt="" class="wp-image-3052" /><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">The SkyKeeper battle management component marketed with the IRIS-T SL and Giraffe 4A radar in collaboration with Diehl and SAAB in 2019. Graphic: Lockheed Martin</figcaption></figure>
<!-- /wp:image -->

<!-- wp:quote -->
<blockquote class="wp-block-quote"><!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>An IRIS-T fire unit consists of the 3D radar, a tactical operations centre and three eight-unit launchers as well as auxiliary vehicles for maintenance and loading.</em> <em>The application-dependent range is up to 40 km and is to be increased to 80 km with IRIS-T SLX system. </em></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>—</em>Diehl Defence</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph --></blockquote>
<!-- /wp:quote -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>NASAMS</strong>: Norwegian (or National) Advanced Surface-to-Air Missile System.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Developed jointly by Raytheon and Kongsberg Defence &amp; Aerospace, and is a network‐centric, modular, and distributed medium‐range air defence system. Using the air-to-air AIM-120 AMRAAM missile in a ground-launched role.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:gallery {"linkTo":"none"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-gallery has-nested-images columns-default is-cropped"><!-- wp:image {"id":3028,"sizeSlug":"large","linkDestination":"none"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img src="https://norskluftvern.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/img_7349-1.jpg?w=851" alt="" class="wp-image-3028" /><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">System components</figcaption></figure>
<!-- /wp:image -->

<!-- wp:image {"id":3027,"sizeSlug":"large","linkDestination":"none"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img src="https://norskluftvern.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/img_7350.jpg?w=850" alt="" class="wp-image-3027" /><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Fire Distribution Center</figcaption></figure>
<!-- /wp:image --><figcaption class="blocks-gallery-caption wp-element-caption">Graphics: Kongsberg</figcaption></figure>
<!-- /wp:gallery -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>NASAMS features multiple dispersed launchers, radars, and a central Fire Distribution Center (FDC) that links sensors and shooters over wide areas, coordinates engagement management, target allocation, and integrates data via multiple datalinks.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The system use electronically scanned radars such as the Raytheon <a href="https://www.rtx.com/raytheon/what-we-do/land/sentinel-radar" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Sentinel</a> or potentially the newer medium-range radars like the <a href="https://norskluftvern.com/2025/04/03/ghosteye-mr-ny-multifunksjonsradar-med-middels-rekkevidde-og-360-dekning/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">GhostEye MR</a>, as well as TV/IR cameras.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:quote -->
<blockquote class="wp-block-quote"><!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>Normally, a number of NASAMS fire units are netted together in a uniquely designed ”hard-realtime” communication network to ensure minimum latency over large distances for maximum system performance utilizing the unique capabilities of the AMRAAM missile</em></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>—Kongsberg</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph --></blockquote>
<!-- /wp:quote -->

<!-- wp:quote -->
<blockquote class="wp-block-quote"><!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>In October 2021, Raytheon unveiled a new medium-range air and missile defense radar for NASAMS called the&nbsp;<a href="https://www.rtx.com/raytheon/what-we-do/integrated-air-and-missile-defense/ghosteye-mr" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">GhostEye MR</a>&nbsp;radar.</em></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>—Raytheon</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph --></blockquote>
<!-- /wp:quote -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Range &amp; Effectiveness</strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>IRIS-T SL</strong>. The SLS variant is optimized for very short-range defense (approximately 12 km range, lower altitude coverage), while the SLM variant achieves medium-range performance with ranges up to about 40 km and ceilings around 20 km.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:gallery {"linkTo":"none"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-gallery has-nested-images columns-default is-cropped"><!-- wp:image {"id":3022,"sizeSlug":"large","linkDestination":"none"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img src="https://norskluftvern.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/img_7347-1.jpg?w=1024" alt="" class="wp-image-3022" /></figure>
<!-- /wp:image -->

<!-- wp:image {"id":3021,"sizeSlug":"large","linkDestination":"none"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img src="https://norskluftvern.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/img_7346-1.jpg?w=1024" alt="" class="wp-image-3021" /></figure>
<!-- /wp:image --><figcaption class="blocks-gallery-caption wp-element-caption">IRIS-T ground-based air defence (Graphics: Diehl Defence)</figcaption></figure>
<!-- /wp:gallery -->

<!-- wp:quote -->
<blockquote class="wp-block-quote"><!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>In October 2022, when the enemy massively attacked objects of critical infrastructure in Ukraine, the SAM shot down 100 percent of the targets</em>. —Ukrainian IRIS-T SL operator «Kum»</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph --></blockquote>
<!-- /wp:quote -->

<!-- wp:quote -->
<blockquote class="wp-block-quote"><!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>We have received confirmation that IRIS-T SLM exceeds even the highest expectations in terms of hit accuracy, reliability and lethality even over long distances and in adverse weather conditions. —</em>H. Rauch CEO Diehl Defence, March 2023</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph --></blockquote>
<!-- /wp:quote -->

<!-- wp:quote -->
<blockquote class="wp-block-quote"><!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>… it has served as a bulwark against the countless missiles that Russia shoots at Ukraine every day, having shot down over 250 projectiles of various sorts… a hit rate of 95%.</em></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>—Chancellor Olaf Scholz, September 2024</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph --></blockquote>
<!-- /wp:quote -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>NASAMS</strong>. Depending on the missile variant employed, NASAMS can engage targets at various ranges from a few kilometers to several tens of kilometers, and well into the medium range category when employing extended-range interceptors.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:quote -->
<blockquote class="wp-block-quote"><!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>Featuring an enlarged rocket motor and other enhancements, <a href="https://www.rtx.com/raytheon/what-we-do/air/amraam-missile" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">AMRAAM-ER</a> will greatly expand the NASAMS engagement envelope with a 50 percent increase in maximum range and 70 percent increase in maximum altitude. </em></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>—Raytheon, October 2016</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph --></blockquote>
<!-- /wp:quote -->

<!-- wp:quote -->
<blockquote class="wp-block-quote"><!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>About 900 missiles and drones have been shot down by NASAMS, with a hit rate of 94. That is a very, very high number</em>. </p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>—Head of the Royal Norwegian Air Force's support to Ukraine, February 2025</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph --></blockquote>
<!-- /wp:quote -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Use Cases, Mobility &amp; Deployments</strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>IRIS-T SL </strong>batteries are well suited for 360° point defence, as well as part of a layered air defense network in cooperation with longer-range systems - or dispersed while protecting moving battlegroups. The system with containerized and truck-mounted launchers, radars and command units offer rapid reaction times - combat ready postures are achieved minutes after relocation.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Although the IRIS-T SL is a relatively new system compared to NASAMS, it has been acquired by several countries, including Ukraine. See <a href="https://norskluftvern.com/2025/02/09/iris-t-sl/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">IRIS-T SL</a> for extensive list of user countries. </p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:image {"id":3031,"sizeSlug":"large"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img src="https://norskluftvern.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/img_7352-1.jpg?w=480" alt="" class="wp-image-3031" /><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">IRIS-T battery on the move (Image: Diehl Defence)</figcaption></figure>
<!-- /wp:image -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>NASAMS</strong> is a network‐centric, modular, and distributed system that features multiple dispersed launchers, radars, and a central FDC that links sensors and shooters over wide areas.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>NASAMS leverages a network‐centric and modular design. This distributed concept allows for radars, launchers and command posts to be separated by tens of kilometers thus enhancing survivability while enabling a 360° and wide-area key assets protection - remaining linked and coordinated by a central FDC.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Components are typically vehicle-mounted, often on trucks or trailers, and suited for rapid relocation, enabling both static base defence and more dispersed operations.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:quote -->
<blockquote class="wp-block-quote"><!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>NASAMS is by nature a highly mobile system designed with a focus on operational flexibility for protection of Air Bases, Sea Ports, Populated Areas, other High-Value Assets and Army Forces.</em> <em>The launcher has a short into-action-time and can either be emplaced on the ground before firing or be fired from truck.</em> —Kongsberg</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph --></blockquote>
<!-- /wp:quote -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>As of 2024, NASAMS is deployed by 13+ countries, amongst others Norway, the United States, Spain, the Netherlands, Finland, and Ukraine.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Future developments</strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>IRIS-T SL</strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:quote -->
<blockquote class="wp-block-quote"><!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>Diehl Defence is constantly developing its systems to face emerging threat scenarios. The range is to be increased to 80 km with IRIS-T SLX system. </em> —Diehl Defence</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph --></blockquote>
<!-- /wp:quote -->

<!-- wp:image {"id":3023,"sizeSlug":"large"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img src="https://norskluftvern.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/img_7348-1.jpg?w=1024" alt="" class="wp-image-3023" /><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">IRIS-T SLX longer-range missile (Graphic: Diehl Defence)</figcaption></figure>
<!-- /wp:image -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>NASAMS</strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:quote -->
<blockquote class="wp-block-quote"><!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>NASAMS is designed to evolve with the development of technology and can integrate with or utilize future technology when available. This can be future active or passive radars and sensors, Sense &amp; Warn capability, and a wide range of effectors, like C-RAM, C-UAS and the Raytheon Family of Missiles; Aim 9X Sidewinder Block II, AIM-120 AMRAAM an AMRAAM ER. The evolution is enabled by the open architecture in the FDC.</em> —Kongsberg</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph --></blockquote>
<!-- /wp:quote -->

<!-- wp:image {"id":3018,"sizeSlug":"large"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img src="https://norskluftvern.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/img_7331.jpg?w=850" alt="" class="wp-image-3018" /><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">The Kongsberg Multi Missile Launcher showcasing three classes of missiles. Image: KONGSBERG</figcaption></figure>
<!-- /wp:image -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>More on</strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:list -->
<ul class="wp-block-list"><!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><a href="https://norskluftvern.com/2025/02/09/iris-t-sl/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">IRIS-T SL</a></li>
<!-- /wp:list-item -->

<!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><a href="https://norskluftvern.com/2025/02/08/nasams-a-versatile-and-modern-air-defence-system/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">NASAMS</a></li>
<!-- /wp:list-item --></ul>
<!-- /wp:list -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Check out </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:list -->
<ul class="wp-block-list"><!-- wp:list-item -->
<li><a href="https://norskluftvern.com/2025/03/18/patriot-vs-samp-t-air-and-missile-defense-systems-comparison/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Patriot vs. SAMP/T</a></li>
<!-- /wp:list-item --></ul>
<!-- /wp:list -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Image collage: Royal Norwegian Airforce / Diehl Defence</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->
