## Small Nation, Global Impact: How Norway's NASAMS Revolutionized Air Defense with 94% Success Rate

**Date:** Wed, 16 Jul 2025 22:21:23 +0000  
**Author:** norskluftvern  
**Type:** Post  
**Link:** [http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/17/small-nation-global-impact-how-norways-nasams-revolutionized-air-defense-with-94-success-rate/](http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/17/small-nation-global-impact-how-norways-nasams-revolutionized-air-defense-with-94-success-rate/)

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>In an era where aerial threats have evolved from traditional aircraft to sophisticated cruise missiles, hypersonic weapons, and drone swarms, the need for adaptive, effective air defense systems has never been more critical. While major military powers have developed expensive, complex surface-to-air missile systems, a small Nordic nation has quietly revolutionized the field through innovative thinking and strategic partnership. <strong>Norway’s development of NASAMS proves that small nations can revolutionize global military technology through innovative engineering and strategic partnerships, as evidenced by the system’s unprecedented 94% combat effectiveness rate in Ukraine and its adoption by 13 countries including the United States for protecting Washington D.C.</strong> This achievement challenges the conventional assumption that only superpowers can develop world-changing defense technologies, demonstrating instead how focused innovation and international collaboration can produce systems that become the global standard for air defense.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Norwegian innovation transforms air defense architecture</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The genesis of NASAMS in the 1980s reflected Norway’s strategic imperative to protect its southern air bases while maximizing limited defense resources. Rather than purchasing expensive, dedicated surface-to-air missiles, Norwegian engineers at Kongsberg Defence &amp; Aerospace pursued a groundbreaking concept: adapting the AIM-120 AMRAAM air-to-air missile for ground-based launch. This seemingly simple innovation required extensive engineering to develop the Surface-Launched AMRAAM (SL-AMRAAM), creating the world’s first system to successfully repurpose fighter aircraft missiles for terrestrial defense. The Norwegian Solution (NORSOL), implemented by the late 1990s, integrated NASAMS with 40mm Bofors guns and RBS 70 MANPADS systems, creating a <strong>layered defense network</strong> that could engage threats from multiple ranges while preventing both overkill and underkill scenarios through coordinated command and control.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:embed {"url":"https://youtu.be/M-_rUm0fsGA?si=FX-UE_U7BbKnLK_a","type":"video","providerNameSlug":"youtube","responsive":true,"className":"wp-embed-aspect-16-9 wp-has-aspect-ratio"} -->
<figure class="wp-block-embed is-type-video is-provider-youtube wp-block-embed-youtube wp-embed-aspect-16-9 wp-has-aspect-ratio"><div class="wp-block-embed__wrapper">
https://youtu.be/M-_rUm0fsGA?si=FX-UE_U7BbKnLK_a
</div><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">The Norwegian Solution (NORSOL), implemented by the late 1990s, integrated NASAMS with 40mm Bofors guns and RBS 70 MANPADS systems, creating a <strong>layered defense network</strong> that could engage threats from multiple ranges while preventing both overkill and underkill scenarios through coordinated command and control.</figcaption></figure>
<!-- /wp:embed -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The system’s modular architecture reflected Norwegian engineering philosophy: distributed, networked components that could operate independently while sharing information across vast distances. Unlike traditional air defense systems that required concentrated, vulnerable installations, NASAMS launchers could be deployed <strong>20+ kilometers from control centers</strong>, connected by hard-real-time networks with minimal latency. This distributed approach not only enhanced survivability but also allowed flexible deployment across Norway’s challenging Arctic terrain, establishing a technological foundation that would prove globally relevant.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Technical superiority through strategic simplification</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>NASAMS’ technical excellence stems from its elegant simplicity rather than complexity. The system employs the Raytheon AN/MPQ-64F1 Sentinel Radar, for 3D phased array surveillance, while the Fire Distribution Center provides Norwegian-developed battle management capabilities that coordinate engagements across multiple platforms. Each Multi-missile Canister Launcher carries up to six missiles, creating substantial firepower density in a mobile, air-liftable package that can be rapidly deployed and repositioned as tactical situations evolve.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The evolution from NASAMS 1 to the current NASAMS 3 demonstrates continuous technological advancement. While early systems relied primarily on AIM-120 AMRAAM missiles positioning the system firmly in the short-to-medium range category, the current version of NASAMS incorporates the AMRAAM-ER (Extended Range) moving the system comfortably into the medium range class and with a 70% increased altitude capability at that, plus AIM-9X Sidewinder Block II missiles for precision short-range engagement. This multi-missile capability allows operators to select optimal weapons for specific threats, maximizing cost-effectiveness while ensuring engagement success. The system’s <strong>beyond visual range (BVR)</strong> capabilities enable long-distance threat neutralization, while electro-optical/infrared sensors provide passive engagement options that cannot be detected or jammed by enemy electronic warfare systems.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Combat proven effectiveness validates Norwegian design philosophy</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The ultimate validation of NASAMS’ design philosophy has occurred in Ukraine’s high-intensity combat environment, where the system has achieved unprecedented effectiveness statistics. Since November 2022, <strong>17 NASAMS batteries</strong> from multiple donor nations have destroyed <strong>over 900 Russian missiles and drones</strong>, maintaining an extraordinary <strong>94% success rate</strong> against diverse threats including Kh-101 cruise missiles, Kalibr naval missiles, and various drone platforms. During the November 15, 2022 missile barrage, NASAMS achieved <strong>100% effectiveness</strong>, intercepting all 10 incoming missiles, while in another engagement, the system destroyed <strong>11 cruise missiles in just 2 minutes</strong>, demonstrating both accuracy and rapid-fire capability.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>These combat results vindicate Norway’s original design decisions: using proven air-to-air missiles reduces development risk while leveraging massive existing stockpiles; distributed architecture provides resilience against counter-battery fire; and network-centric design allows multiple units to coordinate seamlessly. Ukrainian forces have particularly praised NASAMS’ ability to engage <strong>both high-value cruise missiles and small, low-cost drones</strong>, demonstrating the system’s versatility across the threat spectrum. The system’s performance has been so impressive that Ukraine’s success rate with NASAMS significantly exceeds that of other air defense systems, establishing the Norwegian-designed platform as arguably the most effective medium-range air defense system in active combat.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">International adoption confirms strategic significance</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The global embrace of NASAMS technology reflects its strategic importance beyond Norway’s borders. <strong>Thirteen nations</strong> now operate NASAMS systems, including high-profile deployments such as protecting the U.S. National Capital Region since 2005, where the system guards airspace around the White House with what Kongsberg describes as “extreme reliability and very high availability.” Spain, Finland, the Netherlands, Chile, Lithuania, Indonesia, Australia, Hungary, Qatar, and Oman have all invested in NASAMS technology, with contracts totaling billions of dollars and representing one of Norway’s most successful defense export programs.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Recent international developments underscore NASAMS’ growing strategic relevance. Australia’s A$2.5 billion contract represents the largest single NASAMS purchase, while Hungary’s €410 million investment in six batteries demonstrates European commitment to the technology. The Netherlands has ordered additional NASAMS 3 systems, and Taiwan’s approved purchase of 3 NASAMS systems highlights the platform’s importance in contested regions. Romania and Slovakia are considering substantial procurements worth €3.85 billion and €800 million respectively, indicating that NASAMS has become the preferred solution for nations seeking cost-effective, proven air defense capabilities.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Norwegian strategic vision shapes future defense paradigms</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>From a Norwegian perspective, NASAMS represents successful realization of strategic defense objectives: maximizing limited resources through innovation, strengthening international partnerships, and establishing technological leadership in critical defense sectors. Defense Minister Bjørn Arild Gram’s statement that “air defence is crucial for the defence of both Norway and Ukraine” reflects Norway’s commitment to using NASAMS as a foundation for both national security and international stability. The Norwegian government’s <strong>NOK 43 billion allocation</strong> for air defense strengthening through 2030, with NASAMS as the centerpiece, demonstrates long-term strategic commitment to the platform.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Norway’s development of NOMADS (Norwegian Manoeuvre Air Defence System) based on NASAMS technology specifically addresses Arctic operational requirements, ensuring the system remains relevant for Norway’s unique geographical challenges. The ongoing <strong>GhostEye-based radar development</strong> through the NASAMS Capabilities Collaborative Agreement with Raytheon will provide <strong>up to 120-kilometer detection capability</strong>, representing a quantum leap in sensor performance. These investments, totaling <strong>NOK 4.8 billion for new systems and NOK 365 million for radar upgrades</strong>, position Norway to maintain technological leadership while contributing to allied security architectures worldwide.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Norway’s technological legacy proves small nations can shape global security</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The success of NASAMS validates the thesis that small nations can revolutionize global military technology through strategic innovation and partnerships. Norway’s achievement—creating a system that protects the U.S. capital, maintains a <strong>94% combat effectiveness rate</strong> in high-intensity warfare, and has been adopted by <strong>13 nations worldwide</strong>—definitively proves that military technological leadership is not reserved for superpowers alone. The transformation of a Norwegian national defense requirement into the world’s most battle-tested air defense system represents a paradigm shift in how we understand defense innovation.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>This Norwegian success story demonstrates that in modern defense technology, strategic vision and innovative engineering matter more than scale or resources. By leveraging the “triangle of expertise” model—combining military requirements, research institutions, and industry capability—Norway created a system that major powers now depend upon for their most critical defense needs. As NASAMS continues evolving with next-generation capabilities and protecting an expanding global network, it stands as definitive proof that small nations with focused strategies can not only contribute to international security but can fundamentally reshape how the world approaches defense challenges.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>AI-assisted article. </p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

## THAAD Missile Defense System: Cost Analysis and Performance Comparison

**Date:** Fri, 18 Jul 2025 23:10:20 +0000  
**Author:** norskluftvern  
**Type:** Post  
**Link:** [http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/thaad-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/](http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/thaad-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/)

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Strategic Defense at Premium Pricing</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>THAAD (Terminal High Altitude Area Defense) commands $12.7 million per interceptor in 2024-2025, positioning it as a mid-tier option between the ultra-premium SM-3 Block IIA and more affordable Patriot PAC-3 MSE.</strong> This pricing reflects its unique capability to intercept threats in both atmospheric and exo-atmospheric environments, a technical feat that no other mobile system can match. Recent combat deployments in Israel and growing international demand validate customer willingness to pay premium prices for proven, versatile defense capabilities.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>THAAD’s strategic value lies in filling a crucial gap in layered missile defense architectures. While the SM-3 Block IIA excels at long-range ICBM interception and Patriot PAC-3 MSE handles shorter-range threats, THAAD bridges this coverage gap with unmatched flexibility and proven combat effectiveness.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"level":3} -->
<h3 class="wp-block-heading">Current cost structure reveals strategic positioning</h3>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>THAAD interceptors cost $12.7 million each</strong> based on the FY2025 Missile Defense Agency budget, representing a 41% increase from previous estimates. This pricing includes production costs of $12-15 million per interceptor, with total program costs reaching $18-27 million when including research and development expenses. A complete THAAD battery, comprising six launchers, 48 interceptors, AN/TPY-2 radar, and fire control systems, costs <strong>$1.0-1.8 billion</strong>.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>SM-3 Block IIA interceptors command $27.9 million each</strong>, making them 120% more expensive than THAAD. This premium reflects their advanced capabilities for intercepting intercontinental ballistic missiles at ranges exceeding 1,200 kilometers. However, their higher cost and complexity make them less suitable for the medium-range ballistic missile threats that THAAD was designed to counter.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Patriot PAC-3 MSE interceptors cost $3.7-4.2 million each</strong>, making them 71% less expensive than THAAD. While significantly cheaper, they offer limited range (20-35 kilometers) and altitude capabilities, making them suitable only for terminal-phase interceptions of shorter-range threats.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"level":3} -->
<h3 class="wp-block-heading">Combat effectiveness justifies premium pricing</h3>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>THAAD’s <strong>100% success rate in operational testing</strong> and recent combat deployments demonstrates superior reliability. The system successfully intercepted Houthi missiles defending the UAE in 2022 and was extensively used during Israel’s defense against Iranian attacks in 2024-2025. Defense officials estimate 39-93 THAAD interceptors were used during a 12-day conflict period, with mission success rates remaining at 100%.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The system’s <strong>unique dual-environment capability</strong> allows it to intercept targets both inside and outside Earth’s atmosphere, providing coverage gaps that neither SM-3 nor Patriot systems can fill. This flexibility enables a single THAAD battery to protect against medium-range ballistic missiles, tactical ballistic missiles, and some intermediate-range threats.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Technical specifications demonstrate advanced capabilities</strong>: THAAD intercepts targets at ranges up to 200 kilometers and altitudes up to 150 kilometers using hit-to-kill technology. The system’s mobility advantage over fixed SM-3 installations and superior range compared to Patriot systems create tactical advantages worth the premium cost.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"level":3} -->
<h3 class="wp-block-heading">International sales validate market positioning</h3>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Major international contracts demonstrate customer confidence</strong> in THAAD’s value proposition. The UAE’s $3.38 billion investment in THAAD systems (2011-2022) represents one of the most successful foreign military sales programs. Saudi Arabia’s $15 billion contract for seven THAAD batteries includes 44 launchers, 360 interceptors, and seven AN/TPY-2 radars, with local manufacturing partnerships reducing long-term costs.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Recent pricing trends show sustained demand</strong>: Qatar’s $42 billion military package includes THAAD systems, while Lockheed Martin received a $2.8 billion contract for THAAD 6.0 development through 2035. The company delivered its 900th interceptor in January 2025, with production scaling to meet growing international demand.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Twenty-year lifecycle costs</strong> for a complete THAAD battery range from $2-3 billion, including operations, sustainment, and interceptor replenishment. This compares favorably to fixed SM-3 installations when considering mobility advantages and reduced infrastructure requirements.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading {"level":3} -->
<h3 class="wp-block-heading">Strategic value proposition drives customer decisions</h3>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>THAAD fills critical capability gaps</strong> in regional missile defense architectures. Iran’s expanding ballistic missile arsenal, including medium-range systems that exceed Patriot capabilities but don’t require SM-3’s full ICBM-class performance, creates demand for THAAD’s specific capability set.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Operational flexibility justifies costs</strong> for many customers. Unlike fixed SM-3 installations, THAAD batteries can be rapidly deployed to respond to emerging threats or support expeditionary operations. This mobility advantage proved crucial during recent Middle East deployments.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Interoperability benefits</strong> enable THAAD to integrate with existing Aegis, Patriot, and other defense systems, creating layered defense architectures that maximize cost-effectiveness across different threat scenarios.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>THAAD’s position as the world’s most advanced mobile missile defense system, combined with its proven combat record and unique technical capabilities, justifies its premium pricing in an increasingly complex threat environment. The system’s ability to bridge capability gaps between shorter-range and strategic defense systems makes it an essential component of modern missile defense architectures.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Image: The U.S. Army Ralph Scott/Missile Defense Agency/U.S. Department of Defense</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->

<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

## Arrow-3 Missile Defense System: Cost Analysis and Performance Comparison

**Date:** Fri, 18 Jul 2025 23:35:33 +0000  
**Author:** norskluftvern  
**Type:** Post  
**Link:** [http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/arrow-3-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/](http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/arrow-3-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/)

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Exo-Atmospheric Defense with Competitive Pricing</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Arrow-3 interceptors cost approximately $4 million each, making them 86% less expensive than competing SM-3 Block IIA systems while offering superior range and proven combat effectiveness.</strong> This significant cost advantage, combined with recent combat successes defending Israel against Iranian ballistic missile attacks, positions Arrow-3 as the most cost-effective exo-atmospheric defense system available for export. Germany’s $3.5 billion procurement represents Israel’s largest defense export and validates international confidence in the system’s capabilities.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Arrow-3 represents a paradigm shift in high-altitude missile defense, offering strategic-level capabilities at tactical-level pricing. The system’s ability to intercept targets at ranges exceeding 2,400 kilometers while maintaining combat-proven reliability makes it attractive to nations seeking ICBM defense without the premium costs of American alternatives.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Cost analysis reveals significant advantages</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Arrow-3 interceptors cost $4 million each</strong> based on recent Israeli defense budget allocations and industry analysis, though some estimates suggest costs may reach $30-35 million when including full development expenses. This pricing represents exceptional value compared to competing systems, with complete battery costs estimated at $170 million including launchers, radar, and command systems.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>SM-3 Block IIA interceptors cost $27.9 million each</strong>, making them 600% more expensive than Arrow-3. While both systems offer exo-atmospheric interception capabilities, Arrow-3’s superior range (2,400 km vs. 1,200 km) and combat-proven performance make the cost differential difficult to justify for most customers.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>David’s Sling interceptors cost $1 million each</strong>, making them 75% less expensive than Arrow-3. However, David’s Sling’s limited range (40-300 km) and atmospheric-only interception capability make it unsuitable for the strategic ballistic missile threats that Arrow-3 was designed to counter.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Combat performance validates design approach</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Arrow-3’s operational record demonstrates exceptional effectiveness</strong> since entering service. The system achieved its first operational interception in November 2023, successfully engaging a Houthi missile near Eilat. During major Iranian attacks in April and October 2024, Arrow-3 interceptors achieved what Israeli officials describe as “very high success rates” approaching 99% effectiveness in some scenarios.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Recent combat usage provides real-world validation</strong>: During a 12-day conflict period in 2024-2025, at least 34 Arrow-3 interceptors were used against various ballistic missile threats. The system’s ability to engage targets before their destination is known provides tactical advantages unavailable in other systems.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Technical specifications exceed competitor capabilities</strong>: Arrow-3’s 2,400-kilometer range surpasses SM-3 Block IIA’s advertised 1,200-kilometer capability, while its hypersonic speed and advanced maneuvering capabilities enable interception of sophisticated threats. The system’s anti-satellite capability, demonstrated through sensors that can pivot 90 degrees, provides additional strategic value.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">International market success demonstrates commercial viability</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Germany’s $3.5 billion Arrow-3 contract</strong> represents validation of the system’s export potential and competitive pricing. This deal opens European markets for Israeli defense technology and demonstrates NATO allies’ confidence in Arrow-3’s capabilities versus American alternatives.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Recent Israeli contracts show scaling production</strong>: A multi-billion shekel contract signed in December 2024 with Israel Aerospace Industries expands Arrow-3 production capacity to meet growing domestic and international demand. The U.S. has invested $1.2 billion in Arrow-3 research and development, with Boeing providing 40-50% of production content.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Manufacturing partnerships enable cost control</strong>: The joint Israeli-American production model spreads development costs across multiple customers while maintaining competitive pricing. Key components from Boeing, Stark Aerospace, and other American suppliers provide supply chain security while supporting competitive cost structures.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Strategic advantages justify market positioning</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Arrow-3 occupies a unique market niche</strong> as the only exo-atmospheric interceptor available for export. American SM-3 systems face export restrictions that limit international sales, while other systems lack strategic-level capabilities.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Range superiority provides tactical advantages</strong>: Arrow-3’s 2,400-kilometer range enables interception of intermediate-range ballistic missiles and ICBMs at distances that provide multiple engagement opportunities. This capability is particularly valuable for smaller nations that need maximum defensive coverage.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Twenty-year lifecycle costs</strong> for Arrow-3 systems benefit from Israeli defense industry efficiency and American component supply chains. The system’s proven reliability and combat effectiveness reduce long-term sustainment costs compared to less mature alternatives.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Recent challenges highlight ongoing development needs</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Operational challenges demonstrate system limits</strong>: A May 2025 failure to intercept a Houthi missile near Ben Gurion Airport highlighted the evolving threat environment. Houthi claims of deploying “Palestine-2” hypersonic missiles with Mach 16 speeds and stealth features indicate the ongoing arms race in missile technology.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Continuous improvement programs</strong> address emerging threats through Arrow-4 development and system upgrades. Israel’s commitment to maintaining technological superiority ensures Arrow-3’s continued relevance against evolving ballistic missile threats. Arrow-3’s combination of strategic-level capabilities, combat-proven performance, and competitive pricing makes it an attractive option for nations seeking exo-atmospheric defense without the premium costs of American systems. The system’s export success and growing international demand validate its position as a cost-effective solution for high-end missile defense requirements.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Image: United States Missile Defense Agency</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>AU-assisted article.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

## David’s Sling Missile Defense System: Cost Analysis and Performance Comparison

**Date:** Fri, 18 Jul 2025 23:43:02 +0000  
**Author:** norskluftvern  
**Type:** Post  
**Link:** [http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/davids-sling-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/](http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/davids-sling-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/)

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Cost-Effective Excellence in Medium-Range Defense</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>David’s Sling interceptors cost $1 million each, delivering 14-86% cost savings compared to Patriot PAC-3 MSE while providing superior range and proven combat effectiveness.</strong> The system’s recent 100% success rate in operational combat, combined with its 300-kilometer range and hit-to-kill technology, makes it the most cost-effective medium-range air defense system available. Finland’s €316 million contract and growing European interest demonstrate international confidence in the system’s exceptional value proposition. David’s Sling represents a breakthrough in affordable precision air defense, offering advanced capabilities at prices that make comprehensive territorial defense economically viable. The joint US-Israeli development program has created a system that combines American reliability with Israeli innovation, resulting in proven performance at unprecedented cost-effectiveness.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Cost analysis reveals exceptional value proposition</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>David’s Sling Stunner interceptors cost $1 million each</strong>, representing exceptional value in the medium-range air defense market. Complete battery costs reach $250 million including radar, fire control systems, launchers, and initial missile loads. This pricing makes David’s Sling competitive with much less capable systems while offering superior performance.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Patriot PAC-3 MSE interceptors cost $3.87-7 million each</strong>, making them 287-600% more expensive than David’s Sling. Recent Norwegian procurement data shows PAC-3 MSE costs of $7 million per interceptor, while US Army procurement indicates $3.87 million per unit. Even at the lower pricing, PAC-3 MSE offers shorter range (160 km vs. 300 km) and higher lifecycle costs.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>NASAMS AMRAAM interceptors cost $1.2 million each</strong>, making them 20% more expensive than David’s Sling despite offering significantly inferior capabilities. Standard AMRAAM provides only 25-kilometer range compared to David’s Sling’s 300-kilometer capability, while AMRAAM-ER variants cost $4 million each - four times David’s Sling pricing.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Combat effectiveness validates design philosophy</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>David’s Sling achieved 100% success rate</strong> in its limited operational combat use, with three confirmed intercepts since May 2023. The system successfully engaged Gaza rockets during its combat debut and proved effective against Iranian missiles during October 2024 strikes. This perfect operational record, while based on limited engagements, demonstrates the system’s reliability under combat conditions.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Technical specifications exceed competitor capabilities</strong>: David’s Sling’s 300-kilometer range significantly exceeds Patriot PAC-3 MSE’s 160-kilometer capability and NASAMS’ 25-kilometer standard range. The system’s 15-kilometer altitude ceiling and Mach 7.5 interceptor speed provide engagement parameters superior to most competing systems.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Hit-to-kill technology eliminates warhead costs</strong> and reduces collateral damage concerns. The system’s dual-mode electro-optical/infrared and radar seeker with datalink updates provides precision engagement capabilities that justify premium pricing over basic systems.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">International market success demonstrates commercial viability</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Finland’s €316 million contract</strong> validates David’s Sling’s export potential, representing the system’s first major international sale. The contract includes delivery through 2030 and was selected over competing systems including potential Patriot options, demonstrating confidence in David’s Sling’s cost-effectiveness.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>German interest worth $600 million</strong> indicates growing European demand for affordable air defense solutions. The system’s NATO compatibility and joint US-Israeli development provide credibility with Western allies seeking alternatives to expensive American systems.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Joint development program reduces costs</strong>: The US invested $1.99 billion in David’s Sling development from 2006-2020, with 50% of components manufactured in America. This partnership spreads development costs across multiple customers while ensuring supply chain security and technology transfer benefits.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Manufacturing efficiency enables competitive pricing</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Rafael Advanced Defense Systems achieved record performance</strong> with $4.8 billion in 2024 sales and $17.5 billion in order backlog. The company’s 64% increase in net profit demonstrates efficient production processes that enable competitive pricing while maintaining healthy margins.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>US manufacturing partnership with Raytheon</strong> provides 50% American content and ensures supply chain security. This collaboration enables technology transfer and potential co-production agreements that reduce long-term costs for international customers.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Production scalability supports growing demand</strong>: Israeli plans for 12 total David’s Sling batteries and expanding international sales demonstrate the system’s ability to achieve economies of scale that maintain competitive pricing.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Strategic advantages justify market positioning</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>David’s Sling fills critical capability gaps</strong> between short-range systems like Iron Dome and strategic systems like Arrow-3. Its 300-kilometer range and medium-altitude capability make it ideal for defending against tactical ballistic missiles, large rockets, and cruise missiles that exceed shorter-range system capabilities.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Twenty-year lifecycle costs</strong> for David’s Sling systems range from $550-650 million per battery, compared to $800-1,200 million for comparable Patriot PAC-3 MSE systems. These savings result from lower interceptor costs, reduced maintenance requirements, and efficient Israeli defense industry practices.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>System integration capabilities</strong> enable David’s Sling to work alongside existing NATO command and control systems, providing seamless integration with Western defense architectures. The system’s mobility and rapid deployment capability add tactical flexibility unavailable in fixed installations.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Competitive advantages drive international demand</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Cost leadership in medium-range defense</strong> makes David’s Sling attractive to nations seeking comprehensive territorial defense without prohibitive costs. The system’s ability to defend 300-kilometer areas with $1 million interceptors provides cost-effective coverage that justifies investment.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Proven combat effectiveness</strong> despite limited operational use provides confidence in the system’s reliability. The 100% success rate in operational combat, combined with extensive testing, demonstrates performance that justifies premium pricing over unproven alternatives.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Technology transfer opportunities</strong> through joint US-Israeli development enable customer nations to participate in production and maintenance, reducing long-term costs while building domestic defense capabilities. David’s Sling’s combination of superior range, proven effectiveness, and competitive pricing makes it the most cost-effective medium-range air defense system available. The system’s growing international sales and proven combat record demonstrate that customers recognize exceptional value in its comprehensive capabilities at unprecedented pricing.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Image: United States Missile Defense Agency</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->

<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

## Iron Dome Missile Defense System: Cost Analysis and Performance Comparison

**Date:** Fri, 18 Jul 2025 23:44:03 +0000  
**Author:** norskluftvern  
**Type:** Post  
**Link:** [http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/iron-dome-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/](http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/iron-dome-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/)

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Precision Defense Despite Premium Costs</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Iron Dome interceptors cost $40,000-50,000 each, commanding premium pricing that customers willingly pay for proven 90%+ success rates and unmatched area coverage.</strong> Despite costing 400-500% more per engagement than C-RAM Phalanx alternatives, Iron Dome’s 4,000+ successful interceptions and 70-kilometer range provide strategic advantages that justify its market dominance. The system’s ability to selectively engage only threats to populated areas, combined with its exceptional combat record, makes it the gold standard for short-range missile defense. Iron Dome represents a paradigm shift from volume-based defense to precision engagement, offering cost-effective protection through intelligent target selection and superior success rates. The system’s proven ability to defend civilian populations while conserving expensive interceptors has created a new category of cost-effective air defense that competitors struggle to match.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Cost analysis reveals strategic value despite premium pricing</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Iron Dome interceptors cost $40,000-50,000 each</strong> for standard production, with operational costs reaching $100,000-150,000 per successful interception when including radar power, personnel, and fuel consumption. Emergency production during surge periods can increase costs to $60,000 per missile, while expedited delivery during conflicts has reached $150,000 per interceptor.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>C-RAM Phalanx systems cost $8,100 per engagement</strong>, firing 300 rounds at $27 each during typical rocket and mortar attacks. While significantly cheaper per engagement, C-RAM’s 70% success rate and 2-kilometer range limit its effectiveness to point defense applications. The system’s $8-18 million unit cost appears attractive until considering its limited coverage area.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Complete system costs</strong> favor Iron Dome’s comprehensive approach. A complete Iron Dome battery costs $70-95 million, providing 100-150 square kilometers of coverage. C-RAM Phalanx units cost $8-18 million each but protect only 1.3 square kilometers, requiring multiple systems for area defense that quickly exceeds Iron Dome’s total cost.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Combat effectiveness justifies premium investment</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Iron Dome’s 4,000+ successful interceptions</strong> since 2011 demonstrate unmatched operational effectiveness. The system achieved 90% success rates during the 2014 Gaza conflict with 735 successful interceptions, while maintaining 90-97% effectiveness during recent conflicts despite facing increasingly sophisticated threats.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Recent combat performance validates design approach</strong>: During the October 2023 Hamas attack, Iron Dome successfully engaged thousands of rockets despite being overwhelmed by the initial 5,000+ rocket barrage. The system’s selective engagement capability conserved interceptors by only targeting threats to populated areas, maximizing cost-effectiveness during sustained attacks.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Technical specifications exceed competitor capabilities</strong>: Iron Dome’s 70-kilometer range provides 50-75 times larger coverage area than C-RAM’s 2-kilometer capability. The system’s all-weather effectiveness and multi-threat capability against rockets, mortars, artillery, and drones provide comprehensive protection unavailable in single-purpose systems.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">International market success demonstrates commercial viability</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Major international contracts validate Iron Dome’s value proposition</strong>: Cyprus paid €104 million for one battery plus five-year support in 2025, while Romania selected Iron Dome for its SHORAD program. The US Army’s $373 million investment in two batteries demonstrates American confidence in the system’s capabilities.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Rafael Advanced Defense Systems achieved record performance</strong> with $4.8 billion in 2024 sales and $17.5 billion in order backlog. The company’s 64% increase in net profit demonstrates efficient production that maintains competitive pricing while supporting continued innovation.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Manufacturing partnerships enable cost control</strong>: The joint Israeli-American production model with Raytheon provides 70% US content across 26 states, ensuring supply chain security while spreading development costs. The East Camden, Arkansas facility produces Tamir interceptors for American forces and allies.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Strategic advantages justify market positioning</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Iron Dome fills critical capability gaps</strong> in modern air defense architectures. Traditional systems like C-RAM provide point defense, while Iron Dome’s area coverage and selective engagement create tactical advantages that justify premium pricing.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Twenty-year lifecycle costs</strong> for Iron Dome systems benefit from the system’s selective engagement philosophy and proven reliability. Annual maintenance costs of $55 million across Israel’s entire fleet demonstrate efficient operational expenses, while mid-life upgrades costing $12 million extend service life by eight years.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Integration capabilities</strong> enable Iron Dome to work alongside existing radar and command systems, providing seamless incorporation into national defense architectures. The system’s mobility and rapid deployment capability add tactical flexibility unavailable in fixed installations.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Future developments enhance value proposition</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Iron Beam laser integration</strong> will revolutionize cost-effectiveness by providing $2-5 direct expenses per interception versus Iron Dome’s $40,000-50,000 costs. The $500 million investment in Iron Beam production demonstrates Israel’s commitment to maintaining technological superiority while reducing operational costs.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Continuous innovation programs</strong> address emerging threats through improved radar discrimination, enhanced interceptor capabilities, and integration with next-generation command systems. The system’s proven ability to adapt to evolving threats ensures continued relevance in changing threat environments.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Export expansion opportunities</strong> grow as regional conflicts demonstrate the value of proven air defense systems. The system’s combat effectiveness and American partnership provide credibility that enables broader international sales despite premium pricing.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Cost-benefit analysis supports customer decisions</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Iron Dome’s market success despite premium pricing</strong> stems from its unique combination of proven effectiveness, strategic coverage, and intelligent engagement logic. The system’s ability to protect large areas while conserving expensive interceptors through selective targeting provides cost-effectiveness that justifies higher per-unit costs.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Customer value propositions</strong> include 90%+ success rates, 70-kilometer range, 100-150 square kilometer coverage, and proven combat effectiveness across 4,000+ engagements. These capabilities justify premium pricing for customers prioritizing effectiveness over initial costs.<strong> </strong></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Strategic importance</strong> of civilian protection makes Iron Dome’s cost-effectiveness calculation different from traditional military systems. The system’s proven ability to maintain normal economic activity during conflicts provides economic benefits that far exceed its operational costs. Iron Dome’s dominance in the short-range missile defense market demonstrates that customers value proven effectiveness over lower initial costs. The system’s exceptional combat record, comprehensive capabilities, and continuous innovation justify premium pricing while establishing new standards for cost-effective air defense through precision engagement and intelligent target selection.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>AI-assisted article.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

## SM-3/Aegis BMD Missile Defense System: Cost Analysis and Performance Comparison

**Date:** Sat, 19 Jul 2025 18:07:14 +0000  
**Author:** norskluftvern  
**Type:** Post  
**Link:** [http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/sm-3-aegis-bmd-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/](http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/sm-3-aegis-bmd-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/)

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>Ultra-Premium Missile Defense at Strategic Scale</strong></h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>SM-3 interceptors command $27.9 million per unit for Block IIA variants in 2025, positioning them as the world’s most expensive ballistic missile interceptor. This ultra-premium pricing reflects unique dual land/sea deployment capabilities that no other missile defense system can match. Recent combat validation during Iranian attacks on Israel demonstrates customer willingness to pay record prices for proven, globally deployable strategic defense capabilities.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The SM-3/Aegis BMD system’s strategic value lies in providing exoatmospheric intercept capabilities from mobile naval platforms, enabling global deployment without fixed infrastructure requirements. While THAAD excels at regional terminal defense and Arrow-3 provides point defense for high-value targets, SM-3 offers unmatched strategic flexibility through integration with the world’s most advanced naval combat systems.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Current cost structure reveals ultra-premium positioning</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>SM-3 Block IIA interceptors cost $27.9 million each based on FY2025 procurement data, representing a 185% increase over the cheaper Block IB variant. This pricing includes production costs of $25-28 million per interceptor, with total system costs reaching $60 million when including Aegis Combat System integration, maintenance, and supporting infrastructure. A complete Aegis BMD destroyer carries an estimated $500 million in missile defense capability.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>THAAD interceptors cost $12.7 million each, making them 55% less expensive than SM-3 Block IIA.</strong> While significantly cheaper, THAAD’s land-based limitation and terminal-phase focus cannot match SM-3’s strategic mobility and midcourse intercept advantages.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Arrow-3 interceptors cost approximately $30+ million each, positioning them competitively with SM-3 Block IIA.</strong> Israel’s system offers superior range (2,400 kilometers vs 1,200 kilometers) but lacks SM-3’s naval integration and global deployment flexibility.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Patriot PAC-3 MSE interceptors cost $4.2 million each, making them 85% less expensive than SM-3.</strong> However, their 35-kilometer range and atmospheric-only capability make them unsuitable for the strategic threats SM-3 was designed to counter.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Combat effectiveness justifies record investment</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>SM-3’s 100% success rate during its April 2024 combat debut demonstrates superior reliability under operational conditions. USS Arleigh Burke and USS Carney successfully intercepted Iranian medium-range ballistic missiles using 4-7 SM-3 interceptors, marking the first combat employment after two decades of development. The system repeated this success during Iran’s October 2024 barrage of 180+ ballistic missiles.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The system’s 85% intercept success rate across over 30 successful space intercepts validates its technical superiority. SM-3’s unique exoatmospheric kill capability provides clean intercepts without debris concerns, crucial for defending populated areas from ballistic missile attacks.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Technical specifications demonstrate unmatched capability:</strong> SM-3 Block IIA intercepts targets at ranges exceeding 1,200 kilometers and altitudes above 100 kilometers using hit-to-kill technology. The system’s demonstrated anti-satellite capability, proven in 2008 when an SM-3 destroyed satellite USA-193 at 240 kilometers altitude, adds strategic value though this remains officially unacknowledged.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The system’s dual land/sea deployment advantage over fixed installations and superior exoatmospheric reach compared to terminal defense systems create tactical advantages worth the premium cost.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">International sales validate ultra-premium market positioning</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Major international contracts demonstrate customer confidence in SM-3’s value proposition. Japan’s $3.295 billion investment for 73 SM-3 Block IIA interceptors represents one of the most expensive foreign military sales in missile defense history. Japan operates seven Aegis BMD destroyers with an eighth under construction, making it the largest international operator.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Recent pricing trends show sustained demand despite record costs:</strong> Raytheon received a $3.33 billion SM-3 contract modification in May 2025, raising total program value from $1.2 billion to $3.33 billion through October 2029. The company invested over $115 million for 67% capacity increases, with facility expansion completing by end 2025.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>South Korea’s three Sejong the Great-class destroyers and planned Canadian acquisition of 15 surface combatants with Aegis integration demonstrate growing international confidence in the system’s capabilities despite ultra-premium pricing.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Twenty-year lifecycle costs for a complete Aegis BMD destroyer approach $2-4 billion, including operations, sustainment, and interceptor replenishment. This represents the highest per-platform investment in missile defense history.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Strategic value proposition drives record-breaking customer investments</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>SM-3 fills critical capability gaps in strategic missile defense architectures. China and Russia’s expanding ballistic missile arsenals, including intermediate-range systems that exceed THAAD capabilities, create demand for SM-3’s specific exoatmospheric capability set.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Operational flexibility justifies costs for strategic customers.</strong> Unlike fixed installations, Aegis BMD ships can be rapidly deployed globally to respond to emerging threats. Current deployments include Europe (Mediterranean/Black Sea), Asia (Japan/South Korea vicinity), and Middle East (Persian Gulf) operations.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Interoperability benefits enable SM-3 to integrate with NATO systems, Army IBCS networks, and allied radars, creating layered defense architectures that maximize cost-effectiveness across different threat scenarios.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Production constraints threaten strategic availability</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Critical procurement shortfalls undermine SM-3’s strategic potential. The FY2025 budget cuts Block IB production from 153 to zero missiles over five years, while Block IIA procurement remains stagnant at just 12 missiles annually through 2029.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Navy Secretary Del Toro and MDA Director Collins both express concern over insufficient capacity: “Given the future threat and our deterrence mission… we are going to need more SM-3s in the future.” Current production levels represent less than one-third of originally planned quantities.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Recent Middle East operations cost the Navy nearly $1 billion in missile expenditures, highlighting the sustainability challenges of ultra-premium interceptor costs during extended conflicts.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Strategic assessment: Ultra-premium pricing reflects unmatched capability</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>SM-3’s position as the world’s most expensive ballistic missile interceptor, combined with its proven combat record and unique strategic deployment capabilities, justifies ultra-premium pricing in an increasingly complex threat environment. The system’s ability to provide global ballistic missile defense from mobile naval platforms makes it an irreplaceable component of 21st-century strategic defense architectures.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>However, procurement constraints and ultra-premium costs raise questions about sustainability during prolonged conflicts, suggesting the need for complementary lower-cost systems to preserve strategic capabilities for highest-priority threats.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:separator -->
<hr class="wp-block-separator has-alpha-channel-opacity" />
<!-- /wp:separator -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>Image: USS Lake Erie fires an SM-3 Block IB during testing. U.S. Navy/Missile Defense Agency</em></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><em>AI-assisted article.</em></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p></p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

## SM-6 Missile Defense System: Cost Analysis and Performance Comparison

**Date:** Sat, 19 Jul 2025 18:49:33 +0000  
**Author:** norskluftvern  
**Type:** Post  
**Link:** [http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/sm-6-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/](http://norskluftvern.com/2025/07/19/sm-6-missile-defense-system-cost-analysis-and-performance-comparison/)

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">America’s Combat-Proven Naval Multi-Mission Shield</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The Standard Missile-6 stands as the world’s only tri-mission naval interceptor, combining air defense, ballistic missile defense, and anti-surface strike capabilities in a single weapon system. <strong>Combat-validated through 80 missile firings during Red Sea operations</strong>, SM-6 has proven its operational effectiveness while securing over <strong>$8.1 billion in international contracts</strong>, establishing itself as the cornerstone of allied naval defense architecture through 2035.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>This revolutionary weapon system represents a <strong>$12 billion program investment</strong> delivering unmatched versatility at <strong>$4.0-4.9 million per interceptor</strong>, with production scaling from 125 to 300 missiles annually by 2028 to meet surging global demand.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Detailed cost breakdown reveals strategic value proposition</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Per-interceptor costs have stabilized</strong> around $4.0-4.9 million for current Block I/IA variants based on 2024-2025 contract analysis, representing a <strong>13.6% cost reduction</strong> through multi-year procurement strategies. The Defense Department’s December 2022 Selected Acquisition Report documents <strong>$11.996 billion in total program costs</strong> for 2,356 planned missiles, with <strong>$7.589 billion allocated for procurement</strong>.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>Recent international sales demonstrate premium pricing but strong demand. <strong>Japan’s January 2025 foreign military sale approval</strong> valued 150 missiles at $900 million, equating to $6 million per unit including training and support. Australia’s comprehensive program totals <strong>$4.7 billion USD</strong> for SM-6 and SM-2 integration across their surface fleet.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The upcoming <strong>SM-6 Block IB variant commands significantly higher costs</strong> due to its revolutionary 21-inch diameter motor and hypersonic engagement capability. This next-generation interceptor began production in late FY2024 with <strong>initial operational capability projected for 2027</strong>. Production investments include <strong>$508.2 million in multi-year procurement savings</strong> and substantial facility expansion at Raytheon’s Tucson, Alabama, and Massachusetts facilities.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Total system integration costs</strong> encompass $934 million in research and development through 2022, with <strong>$1.018 billion in operations and sustainment</strong> over the missile’s 30-year lifecycle. The program benefits from <strong>$333.3 million in recent contracts</strong> with potential expansion to $908.1 million through option exercises.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Performance metrics establish multi-mission superiority</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Technical specifications reveal unprecedented versatility</strong> across all SM-6 variants. The baseline Block I/IA configuration delivers <strong>240-463 km range</strong> at speeds exceeding <strong>Mach 3</strong>, carrying a 64kg blast-fragmentation warhead with dual-mode active/semi-active radar guidance derived from the AIM-120 AMRAAM.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Test success rates validate operational readiness</strong> despite early development challenges. Initial operational testing achieved <strong>58% success rates</strong>, but subsequent refinements eliminated fuze software errors and navigation glitches. Since achieving initial operational capability in 2013, <strong>SM-6 has maintained highly successful test and operational records</strong>.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The <strong>Block IB variant represents a revolutionary leap</strong> in capability, featuring uniform 21-inch diameter construction, dual-pulse rocket motor, and <strong>hypersonic engagement capability</strong>. This configuration enables terminal-phase intercepts of hypersonic glide vehicles, positioning SM-6 as America’s primary near-term defense against these emerging threats.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Multi-mission capabilities distinguish SM-6</strong> from all competitors. Anti-air warfare performance extends to 463km against aircraft, UAVs, and cruise missiles through Cooperative Engagement Capability networking. Ballistic missile defense provides terminal-phase intercept of short and medium-range ballistic missiles, while anti-surface warfare enables strikes beyond 200 nautical miles using GPS guidance.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Recent test milestones include</strong> the March 2025 Flight Test Other-40 “Stellar Banshee,” where USS Pinckney successfully validated hypersonic target engagement using integrated satellite tracking data. July 2025 exercises demonstrated <strong>first land-based SM-6 firing outside the continental United States</strong> via the Army’s Typhon system.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Combat effectiveness proven through Red Sea operations</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Operational validation in the Red Sea</strong> represents the most comprehensive combat test of any modern naval interceptor. Between October 2023 and January 2025, <strong>U.S. Navy ships fired 80 SM-6 missiles</strong> across diverse threat scenarios, establishing definitive combat effectiveness data.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Key engagement milestones include</strong> USS Laboon’s December 26, 2023 intercept of three anti-ship ballistic missiles - the first combat ballistic missile engagement in SM-6 history. USS Carney achieved the <strong>first publicly acknowledged ballistic missile intercept</strong> on January 30, 2024, in the Gulf of Aden. Most significantly, USS Spruance successfully defeated <strong>3 anti-ship ballistic missiles, 3 anti-ship cruise missiles, and 7 unmanned aerial vehicles</strong> in a single complex engagement.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Combat success rates align with test predictions</strong> at approximately <strong>50% single-shot probability of kill</strong>, requiring two rounds per incoming missile according to Vice Admiral McLane. This performance represents “exactly as advertised” effectiveness according to senior Navy officials. <strong>Terminal ballistic missile defense achieved 100% success</strong> in acknowledged engagements, validating the Sea-Based Terminal capability.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Mission effectiveness spans all three roles</strong> during Red Sea operations. SM-6 successfully engaged Houthi anti-ship ballistic missiles in terminal phase, defeated sophisticated cruise missiles with electronic warfare countermeasures, and intercepted diverse unmanned aerial vehicles. The <strong>active radar seeker demonstrated exceptional performance</strong> against electronic jamming attempts.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Cost-effectiveness analysis reveals strategic value</strong> despite high per-engagement costs. The <strong>$344 million SM-6 expenditure</strong> during Red Sea operations represents premium pricing but delivered critical strategic outcomes: protection of international shipping lanes, validation of allied naval capabilities, and demonstration of deterrent effects against regional threats.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">International partnerships drive global naval integration</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Major international contracts total over $8.1 billion</strong>, demonstrating unprecedented allied confidence in SM-6 capabilities. Australia leads with <strong>$4.7 billion USD invested</strong> in comprehensive SM-2 and SM-6 integration across Hobart-class destroyers and future Hunter-class frigates. This historic partnership made Australia <strong>the first country outside the United States</strong> to successfully fire SM-6 in August 2024.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Japan represents the largest single missile purchase</strong> with combined contracts totaling <strong>$1.35 billion</strong>. The 2022 approval for $450 million covered 32 missiles, followed by January 2025’s $900 million authorization for up to 150 additional interceptors. Integration targets <strong>JMSDF Atago-class and Maya-class Aegis destroyers</strong> specifically upgraded to Baseline 9 for SM-6 compatibility.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>South Korea’s $650 million investment</strong> includes 38 SM-6 Block I missiles integrated with Sejong the Great-class destroyers. This partnership positions SM-6 as <strong>a cornerstone of layered missile defense architecture</strong> against North Korean and Chinese ballistic missile threats.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Technology transfer and cooperation</strong> extends beyond simple sales to genuine partnership. All three allied nations upgraded to <strong>Aegis Baseline 9 specifically for SM-6 integration</strong>, while development costs benefit from <strong>Foreign Military Sales customer funding</strong>. Common production lines reduce unit costs and increase manufacturing efficiency across the alliance network.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Additional international interest</strong> includes inquiries from six additional navies, with export authorization granted in January 2017. The system’s <strong>NATO interoperability and alliance integration capabilities</strong> position SM-6 as the preferred choice for allied naval forces requiring advanced air and missile defense.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Strategic positioning dominates naval defense market</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Competitive advantages establish clear market leadership</strong> across multiple performance dimensions. SM-6’s <strong>240-463 km range significantly exceeds</strong> ESSM’s 50km capability, Sea Ceptor’s 25km reach, and even ASTER 30’s 120km maximum. The <strong>tri-mission capability remains unique</strong> among naval interceptors, eliminating the need for separate air defense, ballistic missile defense, and anti-surface weapons.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Active radar seeker technology</strong> enables over-the-horizon engagement through Cooperative Engagement Capability networking, providing <strong>unprecedented area defense coverage</strong>. Integration with E-2D Advanced Hawkeye and F-35 sensors extends engagement ranges far beyond shipboard radar horizons.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Strategic limitations include premium pricing</strong> at $4.0-4.9 million per unit versus ESSM’s $1.7 million or ASTER’s competitive pricing. The <strong>64kg warhead optimizes for air defense</strong> rather than heavy surface targets, while <strong>single-pack VLS requirements</strong> reduce magazine depth compared to quad-packable alternatives.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Market positioning leverages unique capabilities</strong> rather than competing on cost alone. The <strong>“three missiles in one” value proposition</strong> appeals to navies requiring maximum capability within constrained VLS cell allocations. <strong>U.S. alliance integration</strong> provides operational advantages unavailable from European alternatives.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Production monopoly advantages</strong> include Raytheon’s exclusive manufacturing capability, established supply chains, and <strong>Deep integration with Aegis Combat System</strong>. However, single-source dependency creates potential supply chain vulnerabilities requiring careful management.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Economic efficiency analysis versus competing systems</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Cost-benefit analysis reveals strategic value</strong> despite premium unit pricing. SM-6’s <strong>$4.0-4.9 million cost</strong> compares to SM-3 Block IIA’s <strong>$27.9 million</strong> and SM-2 Block IIIC’s <strong>$2.349 million</strong>, positioning it between dedicated ballistic missile defense and conventional air defense interceptors.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Multi-mission capability eliminates redundancy</strong> costs associated with separate weapon systems. Traditional naval air defense required distinct missiles for air threats, ballistic missiles, and surface targets. <strong>SM-6’s tri-mission design reduces logistics complexity</strong>, training requirements, and magazine allocation challenges while providing superior flexibility.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Lifecycle cost advantages</strong> include the <strong>“wooden round” design requiring no shipboard maintenance</strong> over 30-year service life. Operations and sustainment costs of <strong>$8.3 million annually per missile</strong> remain competitive given the system’s multi-mission capability and proven reliability.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>International cost-sharing effects</strong> demonstrate through multi-year procurement contracts achieving <strong>13.6% savings</strong> versus annual purchasing. Allied funding helps sustain production capacity while reducing unit costs for all customers. <strong>Economic order quantity benefits</strong> include advanced procurement funding enabling supplier optimization and material cost reductions.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Production efficiency investments</strong> in digital manufacturing, automation, and quality improvements drive continued cost reductions. Raytheon’s <strong>six consecutive quarters of growth</strong> through 2024 demonstrate strong financial foundation for sustained production capacity expansion.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Production capacity expansion meets growing demand</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Current production capacity operates</strong> at 125-155 missiles annually across primary facilities in Tucson (74%), Huntsville (19%), Andover (5%), and New Mexico (2%). <strong>Planned expansion reaches 300 units by FY2028</strong> through significant facility investments and automation improvements.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Multi-year procurement program</strong> spanning FY2024-2028 contracts for <strong>825 SM-6 Block IA missiles</strong> with total program planning for <strong>2,356 total interceptors</strong>. Recent contract awards include <strong>$333.3 million in January 2025</strong> with potential expansion to $908.1 million through option exercises.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Industrial capacity investments</strong> include RTX’s <strong>$93 billion defense backlog</strong> supporting sustained production growth. <strong>Digital manufacturing connections</strong> across 34 facilities enable analytics-driven efficiency improvements while <strong>automated production systems</strong> increase yield rates and reduce unit costs.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Supply chain constraints</strong> center on sole-source dependency requiring careful supplier management. <strong>International content</strong> includes UK production components representing 13.5% of certain contracts, while <strong>material inflation pressures</strong> are managed through multi-year advance funding mechanisms.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Procurement sustainability</strong> benefits from <strong>strong international demand</strong> providing production volume stability. Congressional support for <strong>multi-year procurement authority</strong> enables industry investment in capacity expansion while delivering taxpayer savings through economic efficiencies.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Future outlook and strategic recommendations</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Technology evolution plans</strong> center on <strong>Block IB hypersonic capability</strong> achieving initial operational capability by 2027. This revolutionary variant’s <strong>21-inch diameter motor</strong> and <strong>hypersonic engagement speeds</strong> position the United States to counter emerging threats from China and Russia. <strong>Block IAU guidance system upgrades</strong> address obsolescence while maintaining production efficiency.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Strategic procurement recommendations</strong> emphasize <strong>continued multi-year contracting</strong> to achieve cost efficiencies and production stability. <strong>International partnership expansion</strong> should target India for potential co-development opportunities while exploring NATO standardization possibilities. <strong>Technology transfer policies</strong> require careful balance between alliance strengthening and national security protection.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Production capacity planning</strong> must anticipate <strong>sustained high demand</strong> through 2035 given threat environment evolution and allied procurement programs. <strong>Supply chain diversification</strong> recommendations include selective dual-sourcing for non-critical components while maintaining security for sensitive technologies. <strong>Workforce development</strong> investments ensure skilled personnel availability for complex manufacturing processes.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Risk mitigation strategies</strong> address <strong>hypersonic threat evolution</strong> potentially outpacing defensive capabilities. <strong>Alternative development programs</strong> like the Glide Phase Interceptor provide complementary capabilities while <strong>directed energy weapons</strong> offer cost-effective solutions against lower-tier threats. <strong>Industrial base resilience</strong> requires sustained investment despite potential budget pressures.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Strategic integration opportunities</strong> include <strong>enhanced networking capabilities</strong> with next-generation combat systems and <strong>artificial intelligence integration</strong> for improved target discrimination. <strong>Air-launched variant development</strong> following the successful AIM-174B deployment expands tactical employment options across service boundaries.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:heading -->
<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Conclusion</h2>
<!-- /wp:heading -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The SM-6 missile system represents a transformational leap in naval warfare capability, successfully bridging air defense, ballistic missile defense, and anti-surface warfare in a single, combat-proven platform. <strong>With over $8.1 billion in international contracts</strong> and <strong>demonstrated effectiveness in Red Sea combat operations</strong>, SM-6 has established itself as the global standard for advanced naval defense.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Strategic value extends beyond technical specifications</strong> to encompass alliance strengthening, industrial base sustainment, and deterrent effects against emerging threats. The <strong>tri-mission capability eliminates traditional naval warfare limitations</strong> while providing unprecedented flexibility for commanders facing diverse threat environments.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p><strong>Economic analysis reveals long-term value</strong> despite premium unit pricing, with multi-mission capability reducing overall system costs and complexity. <strong>Production scaling to 300 units annually by 2028</strong> positions the industrial base to meet sustained demand while <strong>international partnerships provide cost-sharing benefits</strong> for continued capability development.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>The forthcoming <strong>Block IB hypersonic defense capability</strong> ensures SM-6 remains relevant against evolving threats through 2040, while <strong>continuous modernization programs</strong> maintain technological superiority. Success requires sustained investment in production capacity, international partnerships, and next-generation variant development to preserve America’s naval defense advantage in an increasingly contested global environment.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->

<!-- wp:paragraph -->
<p>AI-assisted article.</p>
<!-- /wp:paragraph -->
